01a_quotes.title: Speed at the Cost of Quality: How Cursor AI Increases Short-Term Velocity and Long-Term Complexity in Open-Source Projects
01a_quotes.venue: 23rd International Conference on Mining Software Repositories (MSR '26)

study_design: We then use a difference-in-differences (DiD) design with staggered adoption [29, 31], comparing repositories that adopt Cursor at different times to a matched control group that never adopts during our observation period.
This quasi-experimental approach uses naturally occurring variation in adoption timing to identify causal effects while controlling for repository-specific characteristics and common temporal trends.
To construct a comparable control group, we use propensity score matching [26] to select 1,380 similar repositories from those never adopting Cursor during our observation period.

Our matching model incorporates the dynamic history of repository characteristics—activity levels, contributor counts, and development patterns over the six months preceding potential adoption—ensuring treated and control repositories exhibit similar observable trajectories before adoption.

To estimate treatment effects, we use the Borusyak et al . [29] imputation estimator, a modern DiD approach designed for staggered adoption that avoids biases from traditional two-way fixed effects models.

Finally, to test temporal interactions between outcomes, we also estimate the impact of changes in code quality on future development velocity (and vice versa) using panel generalized method of moments (GMM) models [22].
task: We estimate the causal effects of adopting Cursor on development velocity and code quality, both of which are considered important project outcomes, are commonly measured in prior research, and are closely tied to perceived overall project productivity [38, 52, 89].
population: By scanning Cursor configuration files across GitHub, we identify 806 repositories that adopted the modern Cursor client, with most of the adoptions happening between August 2024 and March 2025.

To filter non-software, educational, toy, and spam repositories [60, 68], we follow prior work [59, 62, 103] by requiring at least 10 stars at collection time—a threshold achieving 97% precision in identifying engineered projects [84]. This yields 806 repositories with adoption dates between January 2024 and March 2025 that are still available on GitHub at the time of data analysis (August 2025).

The top five primary programming languages in our dataset are: TypeScript (366 repositories), Python (118 repositories), JavaScript (60 repositories), Go (36 repositories), and Rust (24 repositories).

For each month with major Cursor adoptions (August 2024—March 2025), we collect monthly time series from GHArchive [1] for all repositories in the population: age, active users, stars, forks, releases, pull requests, issues, comments, and total events.

For each repository in the treatment and control group, we collect monthly outcome metrics and time-varying covariates from January 2024 to August 2025. This ensures that there are at least six months of observations pre- and post-adoption for the treatment group and abundant comparison observations from the control group in each month, providing statistical power for DiD-based causal inference.

Table 1: Descriptive statistics of the 806 repositories using Cursor, collected at the time of data collection (April 2025). Mean Age (days) 1,002.3, Stars 1,475.3, Forks 215.9, Contributors 19.2, Commits 1,816.6, Issues 1,070.3, Pull Requests 719.6
arms: Following standard quasi-experimental terminology, we refer to the sample of repositories adopting Cursor as the treatment group and the matched "never-treated" repositories not using Cursor as the control group in the remainder of this paper.
Specifically, we define this population as all GitHub repositories with ≥10 stars (matching our inclusion threshold for Cursor adopters).

For each Cursor-adopting repository, we perform 1:3 nearest-neighbor matching (three controls per treated unit). While 1:1 or 1:2 is most common [25, 96], many adopters matched the same control during 1:1 matching, so 1:3 provides higher control group diversity.
This yields a matched "never-treated" group with similar propensity score distributions and pre-adoption characteristics (see Appendix).
outcome_definitions: Commits 𝑖𝑡 : Number of commits in repository 𝑖 at month 𝑡
Lines Added 𝑖𝑡 : Total lines added, summed over all commits in repository 𝑖 at month 𝑡

Static Analysis Warnings 𝑖𝑡 : Total number of reliability, maintainability, and security issues for repository 𝑖 at month 𝑡, as detected by SonarQube's static analysis. We refer to them as warnings, since static analysis can generate false positives [56]; this metric should be viewed as an estimate of the effort required to review potential issues in a project.

Duplicate Line Density 𝑖𝑡 : Percentage of duplicated lines in codebase for repository 𝑖 at month 𝑡. SonarQube's definition varies across programming languages, but usually requires at least 10 consecutive duplicate statements or 100 duplicate tokens to mark a block as duplicate [18].

Code Complexity 𝑖𝑡 : Overall cognitive complexity [32] of code base for repository 𝑖 at month 𝑡. Per SonarQube [32], this metric quantifies code understandability and aligns better with modern coding practices than classic cyclomatic complexity [82].
Both have been used as productivity proxies [ 74, 83, 100] with moderate-to-strong correlation with perceived productivity [89].

Software quality is multi-faceted and difficult to capture with a single metric [36, 51, 71, 90]. Quality can be pivoted on defect density [55], specification rigor [50], user satisfaction [45], or technical debt [47].
statistical_results: Table 2: The Borusyak et al. [29] estimated average treatment effects on treated (𝐴𝑇𝑇 in Equation 2) post Cursor adoption. Commits 0.0260 ∗∗∗ (0.0429) +2.63% (±4.40%), Lines Added 0.2514 ∗∗∗ (0.1063) +28.58% (±13.7%), Static Analysis Warnings 0.2644 ∗∗∗ (0.0511) +30.26% (±6.66%), Duplicated Lines Density 0.0679 ∗∗∗ (0.0448) +7.03% (±4.79%), Code Complexity 0.3481 ∗∗∗ (0.0538) +41.64% (±7.62%)

The models estimate a 55.4% increase in commits in the first month, a 14.5% increase in commits in the second month, a 281.3% increase in lines added in the first month, and a 48.4% increase in lines added in the second month, respectively.
On average (Table 2), static analysis warnings increase significantly by 30.3%, and code complexity increases by 41.6%.
The effect on duplicate line density is insignificant.
All outcome variables pass the heteroscedasticity- and cluster-robust Wald tests [29] at the 0.05 level (i.e., passing pre-trend tests).

Table 3: The dynamic panel GMM estimates testing temporal interactions between velocity and quality attributes. 𝐿,𝑊 , and 𝐶 stand for lines added, static analysis warnings, and code complexity, respectively (see Equation 7). 𝐿𝑖𝑡→𝑊𝑖𝑡 Main Effect −0.000 ∗∗∗ (0.015)∗∗∗, Cursor −0.011 ∗∗∗ (0.033)∗∗∗, Lines of Code 0.845 ∗∗∗ (0.073)∗∗∗, Num. Obs. 14,755 ∗∗∗, Sargan 𝑝 0.248 ∗∗∗, AR(2) 𝑝 0.734 ∗∗∗
A 100% increase in code complexity and static analysis warnings causes a 64.5% and 50.3% decrease in development velocity as measured by lines added, respectively.
The adoption of Cursor results in a 1.84x baseline increase in lines added post adoption.

Thus, the velocity gain from Cursor adoption would be fully cancelled out by a ∼5x increase in static analysis warnings or a∼3x increase in code complexity, according to the dynamic panel GMM estimations.
Notably, Cursor adoption still leads to significant increases in code complexity, even when models control for project velocity dynamics.

The adoption of Cursor leads to a statistically significant, large, but transient increase in project-level development velocity, along with a substantial and persistent increase in static analysis warnings and code complexity.
Projects experience 3-5x increases in lines added in the first adoption month, but gains dissipate after two months.
Static analysis warnings increase by 30% and code complexity increases by 41% post-adoption according to the Borusyak et al. [29] DiD estimator.

Table 4: Model fitting summary of the logistic regression models used for matching in each Cursor adoption cohort. Cohort 202408 AUC 0.8506, Cohort 202409 AUC 0.9137, Cohort 202410 AUC 0.8969, Cohort 202411 AUC 0.9144, Cohort 202412 AUC 0.8907, Cohort 202501 AUC 0.8702, Cohort 202502 AUC 0.8716, Cohort 202503 AUC 0.8281
This yields AUC values ranging from 0.83 to 0.91, indicating high discriminative power in the fitted logistic regression models.

Table 6: The estimated average treatment effects on treated (i.e., 𝐴𝑇𝑇 ) post Cursor adoption from different DiD estimators. Borusyak et al. [29] Commits 0.0260 ∗∗∗ (0.0429) +2.63% (±4.40%), Lines Added 0.2514 ∗∗∗ (0.1063) +28.58% (±13.7%), Static Analysis Warnings 0.2644 ∗∗∗ (0.0511) +30.26% (±6.66%), Duplicated Lines Density 0.0679 ∗∗∗ (0.0448) +7.03% (±4.79%), Code Complexity 0.3481 ∗∗∗ (0.0538) +41.64% (±7.62%)

Naming Conventions Pre Mean 12.15 Post Mean 33.48 Change +21.32↑, Code Hygiene Pre Mean 6.58 Post Mean 22.72 Change +16.15↑, Code Complexity Pre Mean 7.59 Post Mean 22.92 Change +15.34↑, Code Style Pre Mean 14.51 Post Mean 29.27 Change +14.76↑, Data Science Pre Mean 2.10 Post Mean 13.39 Change +11.29↑, React Patterns Pre Mean 9.24 Post Mean 18.75 Change +9.52↑, Type Safety Pre Mean 7.61 Post Mean 16.04 Change +8.43↑, CSS Issues Pre Mean 3.41 Post Mean 9.09 Change +5.68↑, OOP/Design Pre Mean 4.53 Post Mean 7.90 Change +3.37↑, Regex Issues Pre Mean 2.37 Post Mean 5.08 Change +2.71↑, Infrastructure Pre Mean 2.62 Post Mean 5.10 Change +2.48↑, Logic Error Pre Mean 7.57 Post Mean 9.66 Change +2.09↑, Security Pre Mean 1.78 Post Mean 3.76 Change +1.98↑, Empty/Incomplete Code Pre Mean 5.37 Post Mean 6.98 Change +1.61↑, Error Handling Pre Mean 5.28 Post Mean 6.44 Change +1.16↑, Accessibility Pre Mean 11.99 Post Mean 12.77 Change +0.78↑, HTML Structure Pre Mean 1.89 Post Mean 1.63 Change −0.26↓, Resource Management Pre Mean 3.00 Post Mean 2.60 Change −0.40↓, Concurrency Pre Mean 5.00 Post Mean 3.05 Change −1.95↓, API Usage Pre Mean 17.77 Post Mean 13.42 Change −4.35↓
ambiguity_or_caveats: Observable adoption through committed configuration files. Our treatment group only includes repositories committing Cursor configuration files to their git system, but developers can use Cursor without committing such files. Thus, our sample represents repositories with observable Cursor adoption rather than all possible Cursor-adopting repositories. This creates potential selection bias: Repositories committing configuration files may be more committed to systematic integration, have more formal processes, or differ in unobservable ways.

To the extent committed adopters use Cursor more systematically, our estimates may represent an upper bound on average effects across all users. However, if committed adopters are more quality-conscious (e.g., more likely to review AI-generated code carefully), estimates could also be conservative.

Uncertainty about usage intensity and persistence. Even with observed configuration files, we do not know how intensively or persistently Cursor was used in each repository. If a repository has multiple contributors with their own development environment, the presence of .cursorrules only indicates someone experimented, not that all contributors used it continuously throughout post-adoption observations.

Contributors may use Cursor heavily for one feature, then revert to traditional development, without leaving visible traces. Unless we observe explicit configuration removal (rare), we assume continued usage, but this approximates actual engagement. Therefore, our main estimates represent intent-to-treat (ITT) effects: the impact of adopting Cursor as measured by committing configuration, averaging over heterogeneous usage patterns.
Model and version heterogeneity. Our dataset lacks information on which Cursor version or LLM backend each repository used; regardless, we still argue that this does not compromise validity.

Imperfect matching. Even if our propensity score matching process achieved strong performance (AUC 0.83–0.91), it still remains subject to untestable unobserved confounders. For example, factors like developer expertise, team practices, project complexity, or organizational culture may affect both Cursor adoption and the post-adoption outcomes.

Although we include numerous covariates hoping to control these factors latently, we believe perfect matching is generally impossible in our setting [26]. The matching process is intended to strengthen the plausibility of our DiD design, particularly the parallel trend assumption, rather than creating two directly comparable groups.

Contamination from alternative AI coding tools. Another particular concern for our study setting is that repositories in both the treatment and control groups may be using alternative AI tools before and throughout the observation period, with or without visible traces. For example, many developers may use early versions of GitHub Copilot or chat-based interfaces like the ChatGPT web portal [2] without leaving any visible traces in the git repository.

Therefore, our estimates should be interpreted as the impact of systematic Cursor adoption compared to the current state-of-the-practice, in which code completion tools and chat-based AI interfaces may be prevalently used, not the impact of using Cursor with respect to no AI usage at all (the latter is generally not estimable in our observational dataset).

Our results may not generalize to other LLM agent assistants, proprietary software projects, and programming languages beyond the three dominant ones in our dataset (JavaScript, TypeScript, Python)—adoption patterns and impacts may differ substantially in these contexts.

Importantly, our study period coincides with rapid evolution in LLM capabilities, agent tooling, and developer adoption patterns. Results observed may not persist as LLM agent assistants mature and developer workflows adapt.

While the propensity score plot (Figure 5) shows that the treatment and control group repositories have highly similar conditional treatment probabilities, the matched control group is slightly skewed toward lower propensity scores.

Furthermore, the balance checks (Figure 5) indicate acceptable but imperfect matching: While all metrics show acceptable balance between the treatment and control group in terms of normalized differences (|Norm. Diff|< 0.25), there are still observable mean differences between the two groups.
In other words, it indicates that our matching is imperfect and our study setting deviates from perfect randomized controlled experiments.

The divergence between Callaway and Sant'Anna [31] and the other estimators warrants careful interpretation, which may be attributed to several methodological differences. First, as discussed in Section B.2, the Callaway and Sant'Anna [31] estimator estimates treatment effects separately within each cohort before aggregating. In our setting, many adoption cohorts contain fewer than 100 repositories (Figure 2), meaning cohort-specific estimates rely on limited sample sizes.

Combined with the inherent noisiness of code quality metrics (e.g., measurement errors in static analysis tools and high variation in code characteristics), this small-cohort structure likely reduces statistical power to detect genuine effects.

We acknowledge that the lack of large cohorts and clear theoretical guidance on the incorporation of time-varying covariates in DiD limits our ability to definitively resolve estimator disagreements for code quality outcomes; the findings around code quality outcomes should be interpreted with appropriate caution.

It is merely a convenience sample, as an architectural limitation in our SonarQube analysis pipeline prevents us from precisely collecting all warnings and tracking each warning to the version that introduced it. Thus, we only use this sample for descriptive explorations rather than for causal inference (e.g., using a DiD design as in the main paper).

However, it is essential to note that this heterogeneity should not be attributed to the programming languages themselves in our setting. Our intuition is that some form of selection bias (e.g., Go repositories may be larger and more sustained) is driving the heterogeneity we observe here.

Also, our dataset does not sufficiently cover several major programming languages, such as Java, C/C++, and Rust, in which the outcomes of AI coding tool adoption might differ substantially from those in Python and JavaScript projects (the latter have more training data and better LLM performance as of now).

The reality is probably that most repositories in our dataset have a mix of developers using different AI autonomy levels, with most switching to full-fledged agents after it became the default in February 2025.

Note that clear separation of feature availability is impossible in our longitudinal study setting, as it is very likely that early Cursor adopters will also switch to using agents later during our observation period.
Our findings should be interpreted within the specific context of open-source software development, which differs from enterprise settings in ways that likely influence the patterns we observe.

These contextual factors likely amplify the excitement-frustration-abandonment cycle while potentially dampening the quality feedback loop. In enterprise settings, organizational mandates, sunk training costs, and managerial oversight might sustain AI tool use despite user frustration, potentially leading to distinct temporal patterns (as indicated in a recent study [73]).

Similarly, enterprise quality assurance processes—mandatory code review, automated testing requirements, dedicated QA teams, and even dedicated agents to do maintenance work—might prevent proportional technical debt accumulation by catching issues before they escalate.

First, our study captures a snapshot of rapidly evolving technology from mid-2024 to mid-2025, when LLM capabilities, agent designs, and developer practices are improving at unprecedented rates—future tools might be able to address the quality concerns we observed.

Second, our quality metrics, while well-established in software engineering research, may not fully capture the multi-dimensional nature of code quality in AI-driven development. For example, complexity metrics were designed for human-written code; whether they appropriately penalize AI-generated patterns that are mechanically verifiable yet syntactically complex remains an open question.
Third, the open-source context of our study may amplify both abandonment dynamics and quality concerns relative to enterprise settings with mandatory, dedicated quality assurance processes.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical study using a difference-in-differences design with staggered adoption to estimate causal effects of Cursor AI adoption on development velocity and code quality in open-source GitHub projects. The study identifies 806 Cursor-adopting repositories and matches them with 1,380 control repositories. Main findings show transient velocity gains (3-5x increase in lines added in first month, dissipating after two months) but persistent increases in technical debt (30% increase in static analysis warnings, 41% increase in code complexity). Panel GMM models reveal that accumulated technical debt subsequently reduces future velocity. The paper includes extensive robustness checks and alternative estimator comparisons in the appendix.

01_extraction.title: Speed at the Cost of Quality: How Cursor AI Increases Short-Term Velocity and Long-Term Complexity in Open-Source Projects
01_extraction.venue: 23rd International Conference on Mining Software Repositories (MSR '26)
01_extraction.study_design_description: A difference-in-differences (DiD) design with staggered adoption comparing repositories that adopt Cursor at different times to a matched control group that never adopts during the observation period. The study uses propensity score matching to select 1,380 similar repositories from those never adopting Cursor, incorporating dynamic history of repository characteristics over six months preceding potential adoption. Treatment effects are estimated using the Borusyak et al. imputation estimator, a modern DiD approach designed for staggered adoption. Panel generalized method of moments (GMM) models are also used to test temporal interactions between outcomes.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Estimating the causal effects of adopting Cursor on development velocity and code quality, both of which are considered important project outcomes, are commonly measured in prior research, and are closely tied to perceived overall project productivity.
01_extraction.population_description: 806 repositories that adopted the modern Cursor client, identified by scanning Cursor configuration files across GitHub, with most adoptions happening between August 2024 and March 2025. Repositories were filtered to require at least 10 stars at collection time to exclude non-software, educational, toy, and spam repositories. The top five primary programming languages are TypeScript (366 repositories), Python (118 repositories), JavaScript (60 repositories), Go (36 repositories), and Rust (24 repositories). Data were collected monthly from January 2024 to August 2025 from GHArchive for all repositories in the population.

arms.description: Repositories adopting Cursor, identified by scanning Cursor configuration files across GitHub with adoption dates between January 2024 and March 2025.

Matched "never-treated" repositories not using Cursor during the observation period, selected via 1:3 nearest-neighbor propensity score matching (three controls per treated unit) to ensure similar propensity score distributions and pre-adoption characteristics.

outcomes.outcome_description: Number of commits in repository at month
Total lines added, summed over all commits in repository at month

Total number of reliability, maintainability, and security issues for repository at month, as detected by SonarQube's static analysis. Referred to as warnings, since static analysis can generate false positives; this metric should be viewed as an estimate of the effort required to review potential issues in a project.

Percentage of duplicated lines in codebase for repository at month. SonarQube's definition varies across programming languages, but usually requires at least 10 consecutive duplicate statements or 100 duplicate tokens to mark a block as duplicate.

Overall cognitive complexity of code base for repository at month. Per SonarQube, this metric quantifies code understandability and aligns better with modern coding practices than classic cyclomatic complexity.

01_extraction.notes: Study uses observational data from GitHub repositories with visible Cursor adoption (committed configuration files), which may represent an upper bound on average effects if committed adopters use Cursor more systematically. Intent-to-treat (ITT) effects reported, averaging over heterogeneous usage patterns. Propensity score matching achieved AUC values ranging from 0.83 to 0.91, indicating high discriminative power but subject to untestable unobserved confounders. Divergence between Callaway and Sant'Anna estimator and other estimators for code quality outcomes attributed to small cohort sizes (many <100 repositories) and inherent noisiness of code quality metrics; code quality findings should be interpreted with caution. Study period (August 2024–March 2025) coincides with rapid LLM evolution; results may not persist as tools mature. Findings may not generalize beyond open-source projects or the three dominant programming languages (JavaScript, TypeScript, Python). Control group may include developers using alternative AI tools (GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT) without visible traces, so estimates represent impact of systematic Cursor adoption compared to current state-of-practice, not impact versus no AI usage. Matching is imperfect with observable mean differences between treatment and control groups despite acceptable normalized differences (<0.25). Dynamic panel GMM models (Table 3) test temporal interactions: 100% increase in code complexity causes 64.5% decrease in lines added; 100% increase in static analysis warnings causes 50.3% decrease in lines added. Velocity gains from Cursor would be fully cancelled by ~5x increase in static analysis warnings or ~3x increase in code complexity.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly uses a difference-in-differences (DiD) design with staggered adoption, propensity score matching for control group selection, and the Borusyak et al. imputation estimator designed for staggered adoption. This is a clear quasi-experimental identification strategy comparing repositories that adopted Cursor at different times to matched controls that never adopted.

per_outcome.operationalization: Count of commits in the repository during the month, extracted from version control system
Total lines of code added summed across all commits in the repository during the month
Count of reliability, maintainability, and security issues detected by SonarQube static analysis for the repository at month
Percentage of duplicated lines in codebase detected by SonarQube (typically requiring 10+ consecutive duplicate statements or 100+ duplicate tokens)
Cognitive complexity score of the codebase measured by SonarQube, quantifying code understandability at month

04_onet.rationale: The study participants are software developers working in GitHub repositories who are adopting Cursor (an AI-powered code editor). The primary task being measured is their development work—writing, modifying, and managing code across various programming languages. While '15-1252.00 Software Developers' might seem appropriate, the study focuses narrowly on the coding activity itself (measuring development velocity and code quality) rather than the full breadth of software developer responsibilities. The work activity 'Working with Computers' best captures the specific computer-based programming work being measured.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI-assisted arm (Cursor adopters) compared to matched control repositories without Cursor. This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment design.
llm_extracted.notes: This is a DiD natural experiment using the Borusyak et al. estimator as the primary specification. The outcome (commits) is count data, and larger values indicate higher productivity (more commits = better). The regression coefficient is reported with SE in parentheses. The paper does not explicitly state log transformation, but the coefficient (0.0260) matches the percent change (+2.63%), which is standard for log-transformed outcomes in DiD models where exp(coef)-1 ≈ coef for small values. The ±4.40% confidence interval translates to approximately [-0.0177, +0.0703] around the 2.63% point estimate. Multiple robustness checks are reported (TWFE, Callaway-Sant'Anna, month-specific effects) but the Borusyak et al. estimate is presented as the primary result in Table 2.

computed.notes: This is a DiD natural experiment using the Borusyak et al. estimator as the primary specification. The outcome (commits) is count data, and larger values indicate higher productivity (more commits = better). The regression coefficient is reported with SE in parentheses. The paper does not explicitly state log transformation, but the coefficient (0.0260) matches the percent change (+2.63%), which is standard for log-transformed outcomes in DiD models where exp(coef)-1 ≈ coef for small values. The ±4.40% confidence interval translates to approximately [-0.0177, +0.0703] around the 2.63% point estimate. Multiple robustness checks are reported (TWFE, Callaway-Sant'Anna, month-specific effects) but the Borusyak et al. estimate is presented as the primary result in Table 2.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a difference-in-differences observational study comparing 806 Cursor-adopting repositories to 1,380 matched control repositories. The study reports only regression-based treatment effects (ATT) from DiD models, not raw means or standard deviations. The reported statistics are: (1) Borusyak et al. estimator: coefficient 0.2644 (SE 0.0511), +30.26% change; (2) TWFE: coefficient 0.1696 (SE 0.0415), +18.48% change; (3) Callaway-Sant'Anna: coefficient -0.1108 (SE 0.1254), -10.49% change. These are log-transformed outcome regression coefficients, not standardized effect sizes. No t-statistics, F-statistics, Cohen's d, or Hedges' g are reported. P-values are given only as inequalities (p<0.001, p<0.01). The total sample includes 21,699 repository-month observations with 4,481 post-treatment observations (Table 7), but these are panel observations, not independent samples suitable for computing between-group effect sizes. Without raw means, SDs, or convertible test statistics, Hedges' g cannot be computed from the available data.
llm_extracted.notes: After re-reading the full paper, I confirm the prior extraction was correct: this is a difference-in-differences observational study with panel data (repository-months). The reported statistics are regression coefficients from DiD models on log-transformed outcomes, not standardized effect sizes. Table 2 reports ATT = 0.2644 (SE 0.0511) for static analysis warnings, interpreted as +30.26% increase. Table 6 shows alternative estimators: TWFE gives 0.1696 (SE 0.0415), +18.48%; Callaway-Sant'Anna gives -0.1108 (SE 0.1254), -10.49%. All p-values are reported only as inequalities (p<0.001, p<0.01, p<0.05), never exact values. The paper explicitly states all outcomes are log-transformed (Table 2 note, page 7). No raw means, standard deviations, t-statistics, F-statistics, or standardized effect sizes (Cohen's d or Hedges' g) are provided anywhere in the paper. Table 1 shows descriptive statistics for the 806 treatment repositories at data collection time, but not at baseline or for the control group. Table 5 shows pre-adoption balance statistics (treatment mean vs control mean for covariates like age, stars, forks), but not for the outcome variable. The sample sizes are: 806 treatment repositories, 1,172 control repositories initially, 1,380 after matching (Table 7), with 21,699 total repository-month observations. These are panel observations, not independent samples. Without means and SDs or convertible test statistics, Hedges' g cannot be computed.
source_quotes: Table 2: The Borusyak et al. [29] estimated average treatment effects on treated (ATT in Equation 2) post Cursor adoption. We log-transform all outcome variables to address skewness and facilitate comparison of treatment effects across outcome variables; after log-transformation, all estimated ATTs can be interpreted as a percentage change of 100(e^ATT − 1)%. Static Analysis Warnings 0.2644*** (0.0511) +30.26% (±6.66%)

Table 6: The estimated average treatment effects on treated (i.e., ATT) post Cursor adoption from different DiD estimators. Similar to Table 2, all outcome variables are log-transformed, and percentage changes are provided for reference. Two-Way Fixed Effects Static Analysis Warnings 0.1696*** (0.0415) +18.48% (±4.92%). Callaway and Sant'Anna [31] Static Analysis Warnings −0.1108** (0.1254) −10.49% (±11.2%)

Table 1: Descriptive statistics of the 806 repositories using Cursor, collected at the time of data collection (April 2025). [Shows only treatment group statistics at collection time, not baseline or control]
Table 5: Balance statistics: treatment versus matched control pre-adoption. [Shows covariates like Age, Stars, Forks, but not the outcome variable Static Analysis Warnings]

Table 7: Summary statistics for all alternative dataset settings explored in this study. Main Settings Original # Treatment Repos 801 # Control Repos 1,172 # Total Observations 21,699 # Post-Treatment Observations 4,481
